title: --userns=ns:<path> appears to be broken for rootless podman
body:
### Issue Description

When using `--userns=ns:<path>` with (rootless) `podman-run` referring to an already existing user namespace (underneath `podman unshare`), I get an error claiming that both, a path and a mapping was specified:
```
Error: OCI runtime error: runc: runc create failed: user namespaces enabled, but both namespace path and non-matching mapping specified -- you may only provide one
``` 
I don't specify a UID mapping in addition, though. It seems that the OCI spec generated by podman contains a default UID mapping despite this option being given.
If I run `podman-create` instead of `podman-run` and then a `podman-inspect` on that container, I do see something like this:

```
...
"UsernsMode": "ns:/proc/284016/ns/user",
"IDMappings": {
    "UidMap": [
         "0:0:1"
    ],
    "GidMap": [
         "0:0:1"
    ]
},
...
```

What seems strange to me, is that this looks like the `--userns=ns:` mode can actually never work if that mapping is generated unconditionally.
I couldn't find any issue, release note or troubleshooting entry that seems related. I might be missing something about this `--userns` mode, but I can't find anything about it other than its description in the manpage, that suggests it's supposed to work exactly like the respective option for joining an existing network namespace.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. 
```
# get rootless user namespace
> podman unshare
```
2.
```
# get a new user namespace underneath 
> unshare --user --map-users 0:0:1 --map-users 1:1:1024 --map-groups 0:0:1 --map-groups 1:1:1024
```
3.
```
# get PID 
> echo $$
284016
```
In a different terminal:

4.
```
> podman run --userns=ns:/proc/284016/ns/user --rm -it docker.io/library/debian:latest
Error: OCI runtime error: runc: runc create failed: user namespaces enabled, but both namespace path and non-matching mapping specified -- you may only provide one
```

### Describe the results you received

An error suggesting a UID mapping was specified in addition to a namespace path, when no such mapping was specified by the user.

### Describe the results you expected

The container to run in the given user namespace.

### podman info output

comments.body:
I attempted to reproduce this on Fedora 42 using Podman v5.7.0-dev from the podman-next repository, but I was unable to. 

@bpoldrack Could you please try to reproduce it on your machine with the latest version of Podman?

A reviewer has determined we need more information to understand the reported issue.  A comment on what is missing should be provided. Be certain you:

* provide an exact reproducer where possible
* verify you have provided all relevant information - minimum is `podman info`
* answer any follow up questions

If no response to the `needs-info` is provided in 30 days, this issue may be closed by our stale bot.

For more information on reporting issues on this repository, consult our [issue guide](ISSUE.md).
@Honny1 The newest available version on debian is 5.6.1. Tried that with the exact same result.

While I'm not really familiar with this code base or even just Go, I do want to point out, that this looks like it's adding a mapping on purpose: https://github.com/containers/podman/blame/ef584d4d6d5b50d7eb48a785be6c954bb0abe5c6/pkg/specgen/namespaces.go#L506

Not touched in 4 years, though. To me the behavior I get looks like `runc`'s complaint is now kind of the other way around.
So, may be the trouble is a behavior change in `runc` since then.

Edit:

Guess, I found it. Here is the change that seems to make the workaround above obsolete:
https://github.com/opencontainers/runc/pull/4124/files
Respective doc change is still an open PR, though:
https://github.com/opencontainers/runtime-spec/pull/1237
Ah, good investigation. Did you try it with `crun`?

@Honny1 I did not. For what I'm building that wouldn't help, b/c I'm not in control of the infrastructure this is supposed to run on. That's why it's all rootless as well.
However, I guess you can take it from here?
